description:
Three design choices worth defending:

1. Fresh ESAPI context per operation (line 74-77 comment, lines 206/255/301/349). The struct comment explains why: tss-esapi Context is !Send, so it cannot be held across spawn_blocking boundaries. Opening a fresh context per call is the correct solution — TPM context creation is a handful of syscalls, not a bottleneck. Attempting to cache the context (e.g. via Mutex<Option<Context>> or unsafe Send impl) would be worse.

2. URI validation with TPM-spec range checks. validate_nv_index enforces the owner NV range (0x01000000-0x01FFFFFF) and validate_persistent_handle enforces the persistent object range (0x81000000-0x81FFFFFF). These catch misconfigured handles at construction time rather than at first TPM call, where the error would be opaque.

3. normalize_signature correctly zero-pads ECDSA r/s components to 32 bytes using saturating_sub arithmetic (lines 407-410). TPM may return shorter-than-32-byte scalars when leading bytes are zero. The right-alignment logic handles this correctly and matches the trait's "64 bytes (r || s), each scalar big-endian, zero-padded" contract.
title: Well-designed: fresh context per operation, validation, signature normalization
